title: Move zeroes to end
description: Move all zeroes to the end while keeping order of other elements

examples.explanation:
Initial: [1, 0, 2, 0, 3], n=5, end=4
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1 (non-zero), continue
Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=0 (zero found)
  Shift elements 2..4 left: [1, 2, 0, 3, 0]
  Place zero at end (index 4): already 0
  end=3, i stays 1 (need to check new arr[1])
Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=2 (non-zero), continue
Step 4: i=2, arr[2]=0 (zero found)
  Shift elements 3..3 left: [1, 2, 3, 0, 0]
  Place zero at end (index 3): already 0
  end=2, i stays 2
Step 5: i=2, arr[2]=3 (non-zero), continue
Step 6: i=3 > end=2, loop ends
Final: [1, 2, 3, 0, 0]

Initial: [0, 1, 0, 3, 12], n=5, end=4
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=0 (zero found)
  Shift elements 1..4 left: [1, 0, 3, 12, 0]
  Place zero at end (index 4): already 0
  end=3, i stays 0
Step 2: i=0, arr[0]=1 (non-zero), continue
Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=0 (zero found)
  Shift elements 2..3 left: [1, 3, 12, 0, 0]
  Place zero at end (index 3): already 0
  end=2, i stays 1
Step 4: i=1, arr[1]=3 (non-zero), continue
Step 5: i=2, arr[2]=12 (non-zero), continue
Step 6: i=3 > end=2, loop ends
Final: [1, 3, 12, 0, 0]

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array, and for each zero found, shift all subsequent elements left by one and place the zero at the end. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Zero Movement
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of moving all zeroes to the end while preserving order of non-zero elements using O(n²) brute force approach.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to moving zeroes to the end is conceptually simple but computationally expensive. The intuition is straightforward: whenever we encounter a zero, we need to shift all subsequent elements left by one position and place the zero at the current end of the array. This preserves the relative order of non-zero elements because we're only moving zeroes, not reordering other elements. The algorithm works by scanning the array from left to right. For each element, if it's zero, we enter a nested loop that shifts all elements from the current position+1 to the end left by one position, then places the zero at the end. We need to be careful with array bounds and tracking the effective end where zeroes should be placed. The time complexity is O(n²) because in the worst case (all zeroes), for each of n elements we shift up to n elements. Space complexity is O(1) as we operate in-place. This connects directly to the key subroutine of shifting elements left. Common mistakes include: not updating the end pointer correctly after moving a zero (leading to infinite loops), forgetting to decrement the loop counter when we find a zero (since shifting changes indices), and off-by-one errors in the shifting loop bounds. The real pattern involves careful index management with nested loops.

examples.trace:
Initial: [1, 0, 2, 0, 3], n=5, end=4
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1 (non-zero), continue
Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=0 (zero found)
  Shift elements 2..4 left: [1, 2, 0, 3, 0]
  Place zero at end (index 4): already 0
  end=3, i stays 1 (need to check new arr[1])
Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=2 (non-zero), continue
Step 4: i=2, arr[2]=0 (zero found)
  Shift elements 3..3 left: [1, 2, 3, 0, 0]
  Place zero at end (index 3): already 0
  end=2, i stays 2
Step 5: i=2, arr[2]=3 (non-zero), continue
Step 6: i=3 > end=2, loop ends
Final: [1, 2, 3, 0, 0]

Initial: [0, 1, 0, 3, 12], n=5, end=4
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=0 (zero found)
  Shift elements 1..4 left: [1, 0, 3, 12, 0]
  Place zero at end (index 4): already 0
  end=3, i stays 0
Step 2: i=0, arr[0]=1 (non-zero), continue
Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=0 (zero found)
  Shift elements 2..3 left: [1, 3, 12, 0, 0]
  Place zero at end (index 3): already 0
  end=2, i stays 1
Step 4: i=1, arr[1]=3 (non-zero), continue
Step 5: i=2, arr[2]=12 (non-zero), continue
Step 6: i=3 > end=2, loop ends
Final: [1, 3, 12, 0, 0]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Left Shift Segment
ladders.desc: Shift a contiguous segment of array elements left by one position.
ladders.explanation: The left shift segment operation is the core mechanical action in the brute force approach. The intuition is simple: to make space for moving a zero to the end, we need to close the gap created by removing that zero. We do this by shifting all elements after the zero one position to the left. This operation preserves the relative order of the shifted elements while creating space at the end for the zero. The algorithm takes an array, a start index, and an end index, then copies each element from position start+1 to end into the previous position. Specifically, for j from start to end-1: arr[j] = arr[j+1]. This overwrites the element at position start (the zero) with the next element, and propagates this shift through the segment. The time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of the segment being shifted. In the context of the full problem, k can be up to n, leading to O(n²) total time. Space complexity is O(1) as it operates in-place. This connects to the core logic of identifying when to shift. Common mistakes include: shifting in the wrong direction (right instead of left), incorrect loop bounds (off-by-one errors), and not handling the empty segment case properly. The real pattern is a simple for loop with careful index management.

examples.trace:
Initial: [1, 0, 2, 3, 4], start=1, end=4
Step 1: j=1, arr[1]=arr[2] → [1, 2, 2, 3, 4]
Step 2: j=2, arr[2]=arr[3] → [1, 2, 3, 3, 4]
Step 3: j=3, arr[3]=arr[4] → [1, 2, 3, 4, 4]
Final: [1, 2, 3, 4, 4] (last element duplicated, zero overwritten)

testCases.input: Array: [a, b, c, d], start=1, end=3

ladders.title: Core Logic — Identify and Handle Zero Element
ladders.desc: Detect when a zero is encountered and determine the segment to shift.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the decision-making logic that triggers the shift operation. The intuition is that we need to identify zero elements during iteration and, when found, determine exactly which segment of the array needs to be shifted left. The core insight is that when we find a zero at position i, we need to shift elements from i+1 to the current effective end of the array (not necessarily the physical end, as zeroes accumulate at the end). We maintain an 'end' pointer that marks the boundary between the processed region (where zeroes have been moved) and the unprocessed region. When we find a zero, we shift elements from i+1 to end, then decrement end (since we've placed a zero at the end). We also don't increment i in this case because the element now at position i (after shifting) needs to be rechecked. The algorithm involves: iterate i from 0 to end; if arr[i] == 0: shift elements i+1 to end left; place zero at position end; end--; else: i++. Time complexity for this logic alone is O(1) per element, but it triggers O(n) shift operations. Space is O(1). This connects to the building block of array iteration. Common mistakes include: forgetting to decrement end after moving a zero, incrementing i when we should recheck the same index, and incorrect calculation of the shift segment bounds. The real pattern is a while loop with conditional index increment.

examples.output: i=1, end=3, trigger shift from 2 to 3
examples.trace:
Initial: i=0, end=4, arr=[1,0,2,0,3]
Step 1: arr[0]=1 !=0, i++ → i=1
Step 2: arr[1]=0 ==0 → trigger shift of elements 2..4
  After shift: [1,2,0,3,3]
  Place zero at end (index 4): [1,2,0,3,0]
  end-- → end=3
  i stays at 1 (recheck arr[1]=2)
Output: i=1, end=3, shift segment 2..3

examples.output: i=0, end=1, trigger shift from 1 to 2
examples.trace:
Initial: i=0, end=2, arr=[0,1,2]
Step 1: arr[0]=0 ==0 → trigger shift of elements 1..2
  After shift: [1,2,2]
  Place zero at end (index 2): [1,2,0]
  end-- → end=1
  i stays at 0 (recheck arr[0]=1)
Output: i=0, end=1, shift segment 1..2

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Iteration with Conditional Index Update
ladders.desc: Traverse an array with index control that depends on conditions.
ladders.explanation: This level addresses the pattern of iterating through an array where the index increment is not always by 1. The intuition is that in algorithms like zero movement, we sometimes need to re-examine the current index after an operation (like shifting), rather than automatically moving to the next index. This requires using a while loop instead of a for loop, with manual control of the index variable. The algorithm initializes an index i and an end boundary, then loops while i <= end. Inside the loop, based on a condition (e.g., whether the current element is zero), we either increment i or keep i the same. This pattern allows us to process elements flexibly. Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration itself, but operations inside may add more. Space is O(1). This connects to the basic operation of array access. Common mistakes include: using a for loop when while is needed, forgetting to update the loop condition (end variable), and creating infinite loops by not incrementing i in some cases. The real pattern is: i=0; while i<=end: if condition: perform action; else: i+=1.

examples.trace:
Initial: i=0, end=2
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1, not zero → i++ → i=1
Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=0, is zero → perform action (e.g., shift), i stays 1
  After action, arr[1]=2 (from shift)
Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=2, not zero → i++ → i=2
Step 4: i=2 > end? No, arr[2]=? (depends on action)
Output: i visited 0 and 2 (1 rechecked)

Initial: i=0, end=2
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=0, is zero → action, i stays 0
  After action, arr[0]=1
Step 2: i=0, arr[0]=1, not zero → i++ → i=1
Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=0, is zero → action, i stays 1
Output: i visited 0 twice and 1 once

testCases.input: Array: [1,2,3], start i=0, end=2, condition: always increment
Array: [0,0,0], start i=0, end=2, condition: never increment
Array: [1,0,1], start i=0, end=2, condition: increment if non-zero
Array: [0,1,2], start i=0, end=2, condition: increment if >0
Array: [1], start i=0, end=0, condition: increment always
Array: [0], start i=0, end=0, condition: increment if non-zero
Array: [1,2,0,3], start i=0, end=3, condition: increment if non-zero
Array: [0,0,1,0], start i=0, end=3, condition: increment if non-zero
Array: [1,1,1], start i=0, end=2, condition: increment if odd
Array: [2,4,6], start i=0, end=2, condition: increment if even

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Access and Comparison
ladders.desc: Read an array element and compare it to a value (e.g., zero).
ladders.explanation: This foundational level covers accessing elements in an array and performing comparisons. The intuition is that arrays store elements at indices, and we can retrieve these elements to check their values. In the context of moving zeroes, we need to access each element to determine if it is zero. The operation involves using an index i to get arr[i], then comparing it to 0 using equality operator (==). This is a constant-time operation, O(1), as array access by index is direct. Space complexity is O(1) as no additional storage is needed. This connects to the concept foundation of arrays and variables. Common mistakes include: using the wrong index (off-by-one), confusing assignment (=) with comparison (==), and not handling empty arrays. The real pattern is a simple if statement: if arr[i] == 0: ...

examples.trace:
Step 1: Access arr at index 1 → value is 0
Step 2: Compare 0 == 0 → True
Output: True (element is zero)

Step 1: Access arr at index 0 → value is 1
Step 2: Compare 1 == 0 → False
Output: False (element is non-zero)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Zero Value
ladders.desc: Understand what an array is and what zero represents as a value.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic prerequisite concept. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements, each accessible by an index (usually starting at 0). Think of it like a row of boxes numbered 0, 1, 2, etc., where each box can hold a value. Zero is a numerical value representing nothing or the absence of quantity; in programming, it's often used as a sentinel or special value. In the problem of moving zeroes, zero is the value we want to relocate to the end. Understanding arrays involves knowing how to declare them, access elements (e.g., arr[0] gets the first element), and that indices must be within bounds. Zero is just like any other number but has the property that it's the additive identity (adding zero doesn't change a number). This foundation connects to accessing and comparing elements. Common mistakes include: confusing array indices with values, thinking zero is a special data type (it's just an integer), and not realizing arrays can hold different types of values. The real pattern is simply working with lists of numbers.

examples.trace:
Step 1: The array has 3 elements at indices 0, 1, 2.
Step 2: Value at index 0 is 3.
Step 3: Value at index 1 is 0.
Step 4: Value at index 2 is 5.
Step 5: Zero is at index 1.

Step 1: Array indices: 0, 1, 2.
Step 2: arr[0] = 0.
Step 3: arr[1] = 1.
Step 4: arr[2] = 2.
Step 5: The value 0 is at the first position (index 0).

testCases.input: Array: [0], what is the only value?

approaches.name: Two Pointer O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers: one to iterate through the array and another to track the position for non-zero elements. Swap non-zero elements to the front, effectively moving zeroes to the end in a single pass.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Move Zeroes to End Using Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Complete solution to move all zeroes to the end while maintaining order of non-zero elements using two pointers in O(n) time and O(1) space.
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires moving all zeroes to the end of an array while preserving the relative order of non-zero elements. The two-pointer approach solves this efficiently by using one pointer (i) to iterate through the array and another pointer (nonZeroPos) to track where the next non-zero element should be placed. As we traverse the array with i, whenever we encounter a non-zero element, we swap it with the element at nonZeroPos, then increment nonZeroPos. This ensures all non-zero elements are moved to the front in their original order, and zeroes naturally end up at the end. The intuition is that nonZeroPos always points to the first zero (or the current position if no zero encountered yet) that can be replaced by a non-zero element. This is a single-pass algorithm with O(n) time complexity since each element is examined once, and O(1) space as we only use two pointers and perform in-place swaps. Connection to L1: This builds on the key subroutine of swapping non-zero elements to the front. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like all zeroes or all non-zeroes, or incorrectly updating pointers after swap. The algorithm works because each swap places a non-zero element in its correct position relative to others, maintaining order.

examples.trace: Initialize nonZeroPos = 0, i = 0. Array: [0,1,0,3,12]. i=0: arr[0]=0 (zero), skip swap, nonZeroPos remains 0. i=1: arr[1]=1 (non-zero), swap arr[1] with arr[0] -> [1,0,0,3,12], increment nonZeroPos to 1. i=2: arr[2]=0 (zero), skip, nonZeroPos=1. i=3: arr[3]=3 (non-zero), swap arr[3] with arr[1] -> [1,3,0,0,12], nonZeroPos=2. i=4: arr[4]=12 (non-zero), swap arr[4] with arr[2] -> [1,3,12,0,0], nonZeroPos=3. End loop, output [1,3,12,0,0].

Initialize nonZeroPos = 0, i = 0. Array: [2,0,4,0,0,5]. i=0: arr[0]=2 (non-zero), swap with itself (no change), nonZeroPos=1. i=1: arr[1]=0 (zero), skip, nonZeroPos=1. i=2: arr[2]=4 (non-zero), swap arr[2] with arr[1] -> [2,4,0,0,0,5], nonZeroPos=2. i=3: arr[3]=0 (zero), skip, nonZeroPos=2. i=4: arr[4]=0 (zero), skip, nonZeroPos=2. i=5: arr[5]=5 (non-zero), swap arr[5] with arr[2] -> [2,4,5,0,0,0], nonZeroPos=3. Output [2,4,5,0,0,0].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Swap Non-Zero Elements to Front
ladders.desc: Implement the core swapping logic that moves non-zero elements to the front while iterating through the array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the essential operation within the two-pointer approach: as we traverse the array, whenever we find a non-zero element, we swap it to the position indicated by nonZeroPos. The intuition is that nonZeroPos marks the boundary between the processed non-zero section and the rest of the array. Initially, nonZeroPos is 0. For each element arr[i], if it is non-zero, we swap arr[i] with arr[nonZeroPos], then increment nonZeroPos. This ensures that all non-zero elements up to index i-1 are placed contiguously from the start. The swap operation is key because it preserves the order of non-zero elements—each non-zero element is moved to the next available front position. Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration, with each swap being O(1). Space is O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is the active component in the full solution; the full problem adds the iteration and condition checks. Common mistakes include swapping zeroes incorrectly or not updating nonZeroPos after a swap. The algorithm works because after each swap, nonZeroPos points to the next position that should hold a non-zero element, which might be a zero or the same element if no zero encountered.

examples.trace: Start with arr=[0,5,0,7], nonZeroPos=0. At i=1, arr[1]=5 (non-zero). Swap arr[1] and arr[0]: arr becomes [5,0,0,7]. Increment nonZeroPos from 0 to 1. Now nonZeroPos=1 points to the first zero after the swapped non-zero.

Start with arr=[1,0,2,3], nonZeroPos=1 (after processing first element). At i=2, arr[2]=2 (non-zero). Swap arr[2] and arr[1]: arr becomes [1,2,0,3]. Increment nonZeroPos from 1 to 2. Now nonZeroPos=2 points to the next zero position.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two-Pointer Technique for Element Segregation
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the two-pointer technique to separate elements based on a condition (non-zero vs zero).
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves using two pointers to partition an array into two segments: one for elements satisfying a condition (non-zero) and another for those that don't (zero). Pointer 'i' iterates through the array to examine each element, while pointer 'nonZeroPos' tracks the next position to place a non-zero element. The intuition is that nonZeroPos always points to the first position in the array that is either a zero or an unprocessed element where a non-zero can be placed. As we move i forward, if arr[i] is non-zero, we swap it with arr[nonZeroPos], effectively moving the non-zero to the front segment, and then increment nonZeroPos to extend the non-zero segment. This technique is efficient because it processes each element once, making it O(n) time and O(1) space. Connection to L1: This logic generalizes the swapping subroutine by defining how pointers interact. Common mistakes include initializing pointers incorrectly or not handling the case when i and nonZeroPos are equal (which results in a self-swap, harmless but inefficient). The technique works because it maintains the invariant that all elements before nonZeroPos are non-zero in original order, and elements from nonZeroPos to i-1 are zeroes or processed elements.

examples.trace: Initialize nonZeroPos=0, i=0. i=0: arr[0]=0, skip, nonZeroPos=0. i=1: arr[1]=2 (non-zero), swap with arr[0] -> [2,0,0,4], nonZeroPos=1. i=2: arr[2]=0, skip, nonZeroPos=1. i=3: arr[3]=4 (non-zero), swap with arr[1] -> [2,4,0,0], nonZeroPos=2. End: nonZeroPos=2, array has non-zeroes up to index 1.

Initialize nonZeroPos=0, i=0. i=0: arr[0]=1 (non-zero), swap with itself -> no change, nonZeroPos=1. i=1: arr[1]=0, skip, nonZeroPos=1. i=2: arr[2]=3 (non-zero), swap with arr[1] -> [1,3,0,0,5], nonZeroPos=2. i=3: arr[3]=0, skip, nonZeroPos=2. i=4: arr[4]=5 (non-zero), swap with arr[2] -> [1,3,5,0,0], nonZeroPos=3. End: nonZeroPos=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating and Condition Checking in Arrays
ladders.desc: Learn to iterate through an array and apply condition checks to identify specific elements (e.g., non-zero values).
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing an array from start to end and checking each element against a condition—in this case, whether the element is non-zero. The intuition is that by examining each element sequentially, we can make decisions based on its value. The algorithm uses a loop (e.g., for i in range(len(arr))) to access each element arr[i]. For each element, we check if it is not equal to zero. This condition check is fundamental because it determines whether an action (like swapping) should be taken. Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration, with each check being O(1). Space is O(1). Connection to L2: This iteration and condition checking form the basis for the two-pointer technique; L2 adds pointer manipulation based on these checks. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds or incorrect condition logic (e.g., checking for zero instead of non-zero). The process works because array iteration allows linear access to all elements, enabling systematic processing.

examples.output: Iteration results: i=0: 0 (zero), i=1: 5 (non-zero), i=2: 0 (zero), i=3: 3 (non-zero)
examples.trace: Start loop i from 0 to 3. i=0: access arr[0]=0, check if non-zero? false (it's zero). i=1: arr[1]=5, check if non-zero? true. i=2: arr[2]=0, check if non-zero? false. i=3: arr[3]=3, check if non-zero? true. End loop.
Loop i from 0 to 2. i=0: arr[0]=1, non-zero? true. i=1: arr[1]=0, non-zero? false. i=2: arr[2]=2, non-zero? true. Done.

testCases.expected: Checks: false, true, false, false, true
Checks: true, false, true, true, false

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Swapping Two Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Perform the fundamental operation of exchanging the values at two indices in an array.
ladders.explanation: Swapping two elements in an array is a basic operation where we exchange the values at two given indices. The intuition is to use a temporary variable to hold one value while reassigning the other, ensuring no data is lost. For example, to swap arr[i] and arr[j], we store arr[i] in a temp variable, set arr[i] to arr[j], then set arr[j] to temp. This operation is O(1) in time and O(1) in space (for the temp variable). It's essential because it allows reordering elements in-place without extra arrays. Connection to L3: Swapping is the action taken based on condition checks during iteration. Common mistakes include forgetting the temp variable (which leads to overwriting values) or swapping indices incorrectly. The swap works because it temporarily holds one value, allowing both positions to be updated correctly.

examples.trace: Start: arr=[10,20,30], i=0, j=2. Step 1: temp = arr[0] = 10. Step 2: arr[0] = arr[2] = 30, so arr becomes [30,20,30]. Step 3: arr[2] = temp = 10, so arr becomes [30,20,10]. Swap complete.
Start: arr=[5,6,7,8], i=1, j=3. Step 1: temp = arr[1] = 6. Step 2: arr[1] = arr[3] = 8, arr becomes [5,8,7,8]. Step 3: arr[3] = temp = 6, arr becomes [5,8,7,6]. Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Understand how to access and modify elements in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basics of working with arrays: accessing and modifying elements by their index. An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, each accessible via a zero-based index. For example, in an array arr, arr[0] gives the first element, arr[1] the second, and so on. The intuition is that indices act as addresses to retrieve or change values. To access an element, use arr[index]; to modify it, assign a new value like arr[index] = value. This is O(1) time for direct access and O(1) space. Connection to L4: Indexing is prerequisite for swapping, which involves accessing and modifying multiple indices. Common mistakes include using out-of-bounds indices (which cause errors) or confusing zero-based indexing with one-based. Mastery of indexing is crucial for all array manipulations.

examples.input: Array: [100, 200, 300], access index 1, then set index 2 to 400
examples.trace: Start: arr=[100,200,300]. Step 1: Access arr[1] -> value is 200. Step 2: Modify arr[2] = 400, so arr becomes [100,200,400]. No other changes.

examples.input: Array: [5, 10, 15], access index 0, then set index 1 to 20
examples.trace: Start: arr=[5,10,15]. Step 1: Access arr[0] -> value 5. Step 2: Modify arr[1] = 20, arr becomes [5,20,15]. Done.

approaches.name: Extra Array O(n)
approaches.summary: Create a new array of the same size, copy all non-zero elements first, then fill the remaining positions with zeroes. This preserves order without in-place modifications.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Move Zeroes to End Using Extra Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution that moves all zeroes to the end while preserving order of non-zero elements using an extra array.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem by creating a new array of the same size as the input. The intuition is simple: we can separate the problem into two phases - first collect all non-zero elements in order, then fill the remaining positions with zeroes. This works because we're essentially filtering out zeroes while maintaining relative order, then appending the required number of zeroes at the end. The algorithm starts by initializing a result array of the same length as input. We maintain an index pointer for the result array starting at 0. We iterate through the input array, and for each non-zero element, we copy it to the result array at the current index, then increment the index. After processing all elements, we fill the remaining positions in the result array (from current index to end) with zeroes. The time complexity is O(n) since we make two passes through the array (one for copying non-zeroes, one for filling zeroes). Space complexity is O(n) for the extra array. This approach is straightforward but not space-optimal. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle edge cases like empty arrays or arrays with all zeroes, or incorrectly calculating the number of zeroes to append. The connection to L1 is that this full solution uses the key subroutine of filtering non-zero elements into a new array.

examples.trace:
Input: [1, 0, 3, 0, 12]
1. Initialize result array of size 5: [_, _, _, _, _]
2. Initialize index = 0
3. Iterate through input:
   - Element 1 (non-zero): copy to result[0], result = [1, _, _, _, _], index = 1
   - Element 0 (zero): skip
   - Element 3 (non-zero): copy to result[1], result = [1, 3, _, _, _], index = 2
   - Element 0 (zero): skip
   - Element 12 (non-zero): copy to result[2], result = [1, 3, 12, _, _], index = 3
4. Fill remaining positions (index 3 to 4) with zeroes:
   - result[3] = 0, result = [1, 3, 12, 0, _]
   - result[4] = 0, result = [1, 3, 12, 0, 0]
5. Return [1, 3, 12, 0, 0]

Input: [0, 0, 0, 5, 0, 2]
1. Initialize result array of size 6: [_, _, _, _, _, _]
2. Initialize index = 0
3. Iterate through input:
   - Element 0: skip
   - Element 0: skip
   - Element 0: skip
   - Element 5 (non-zero): copy to result[0], result = [5, _, _, _, _, _], index = 1
   - Element 0: skip
   - Element 2 (non-zero): copy to result[1], result = [5, 2, _, _, _, _], index = 2
4. Fill remaining positions (index 2 to 5) with zeroes:
   - result[2] = 0, result = [5, 2, 0, _, _, _]
   - result[3] = 0, result = [5, 2, 0, 0, _, _]
   - result[4] = 0, result = [5, 2, 0, 0, 0, _]
   - result[5] = 0, result = [5, 2, 0, 0, 0, 0]
5. Return [5, 2, 0, 0, 0, 0]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Filter Non-Zero Elements to New Array
ladders.desc: Extract all non-zero elements from an array while preserving their order into a new array.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the full solution. The intuition is that we need to separate non-zero elements from zeroes while maintaining their original order. We create a new array (initially empty or of known size) and iterate through the input array. For each element, if it's non-zero, we append it to the new array. This filtering process preserves order because we process elements sequentially from left to right. The algorithm: initialize an empty result list, iterate through each element in input, if element != 0, append to result. Time complexity is O(n) for the single pass through the array. Space complexity is O(k) where k is the number of non-zero elements (or O(n) if we pre-allocate). This feeds into L0 by providing the first phase of the solution - after filtering non-zeroes, we still need to append the appropriate number of zeroes to reach the original array length. Common mistakes include not handling the case where all elements are zero (result would be empty), or incorrectly checking for zero (using 'if element' instead of 'if element != 0' which fails for negative numbers). The real code pattern is straightforward: result = []; for num in arr: if num != 0: result.append(num).

examples.trace:
Input: [1, 0, 3, 0, 12]
1. Initialize result = []
2. Process element 1: 1 != 0, append to result → result = [1]
3. Process element 0: 0 == 0, skip
4. Process element 3: 3 != 0, append to result → result = [1, 3]
5. Process element 0: 0 == 0, skip
6. Process element 12: 12 != 0, append to result → result = [1, 3, 12]
7. Return [1, 3, 12]

Input: [0, 5, 0, 2, 0]
1. Initialize result = []
2. Process element 0: skip
3. Process element 5: 5 != 0, append → result = [5]
4. Process element 0: skip
5. Process element 2: 2 != 0, append → result = [5, 2]
6. Process element 0: skip
7. Return [5, 2]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two-Phase Array Reconstruction
ladders.desc: The technique of building a new array in two phases: first copying selected elements, then filling remaining slots.
ladders.explanation: This core logic represents the fundamental technique used in the solution. The intuition is that we can solve array transformation problems by constructing a new array in multiple passes. First, we copy all elements that meet a certain condition (non-zero elements) to the beginning of the new array. Then, we fill the remaining positions with default values (zeroes). This works because arrays have fixed size, so we know exactly how many positions need to be filled after the first phase. The algorithm: 1. Create result array of same size as input. 2. Initialize write_index = 0. 3. First phase: For each element in input, if element meets condition (non-zero), copy to result[write_index], increment write_index. 4. Second phase: For i from write_index to end of result, set result[i] = default_value (0). Time complexity is O(n) for the two passes. Space complexity is O(n) for the new array. This connects to L1 by showing how the filtered non-zero elements (from L1) become the first phase of this reconstruction. Common mistakes include not properly tracking the write_index, or incorrectly calculating how many default values to append. The real code pattern demonstrates this two-phase approach clearly.

examples.trace:
Parameters: target size = 5, elements to copy = [1, 3, 12], default = 0
1. Create result array of size 5: [_, _, _, _, _]
2. write_index = 0
3. First phase - copy elements:
   - Copy 1 to result[0]: result = [1, _, _, _, _], write_index = 1
   - Copy 3 to result[1]: result = [1, 3, _, _, _], write_index = 2
   - Copy 12 to result[2]: result = [1, 3, 12, _, _], write_index = 3
4. Second phase - fill with defaults:
   - Fill result[3] = 0: result = [1, 3, 12, 0, _]
   - Fill result[4] = 0: result = [1, 3, 12, 0, 0]
5. Return [1, 3, 12, 0, 0]

Parameters: target size = 6, elements to copy = [5, 2], default = 0
1. Create result array of size 6: [_, _, _, _, _, _]
2. write_index = 0
3. First phase - copy elements:
   - Copy 5 to result[0]: result = [5, _, _, _, _, _], write_index = 1
   - Copy 2 to result[1]: result = [5, 2, _, _, _, _], write_index = 2
4. Second phase - fill with defaults:
   - Fill result[2] = 0: result = [5, 2, 0, _, _, _]
   - Fill result[3] = 0: result = [5, 2, 0, 0, _, _]
   - Fill result[4] = 0: result = [5, 2, 0, 0, 0, _]
   - Fill result[5] = 0: result = [5, 2, 0, 0, 0, 0]
5. Return [5, 2, 0, 0, 0, 0]

ladders.title: Building Block — Conditional Array Copying with Index Tracking
ladders.desc: Copy elements from one array to another based on a condition while maintaining order.
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches how to selectively copy elements between arrays. The intuition is that we need to traverse a source array and copy only elements that satisfy a condition to a destination array, while preserving their original order. We maintain a separate write index for the destination array that only advances when we copy an element. This ensures elements are placed consecutively in the destination without gaps. The algorithm: initialize dest array (empty or pre-sized), initialize write_idx = 0, for each element in source: if condition(element) is true: dest[write_idx] = element, write_idx += 1. Time complexity is O(n) for the single pass. Space complexity depends on destination array allocation. This connects to L2 as it implements the first phase of the two-phase reconstruction. Common mistakes include forgetting to increment the write index, or incorrectly sizing the destination array. The real code pattern shows this index tracking clearly.

examples.trace:
Source: [1, 0, 3, 0, 12], Condition: element != 0
1. Initialize dest = [] (or sized array)
2. write_idx = 0
3. Process element 1: 1 != 0 is true → dest[0] = 1, dest = [1], write_idx = 1
4. Process element 0: 0 != 0 is false → skip
5. Process element 3: 3 != 0 is true → dest[1] = 3, dest = [1, 3], write_idx = 2
6. Process element 0: 0 != 0 is false → skip
7. Process element 12: 12 != 0 is true → dest[2] = 12, dest = [1, 3, 12], write_idx = 3
8. Return [1, 3, 12]

Source: [0, 5, 0, 2, 0], Condition: element > 0
1. Initialize dest = []
2. write_idx = 0
3. Process element 0: 0 > 0 is false → skip
4. Process element 5: 5 > 0 is true → dest[0] = 5, dest = [5], write_idx = 1
5. Process element 0: 0 > 0 is false → skip
6. Process element 2: 2 > 0 is true → dest[1] = 2, dest = [5, 2], write_idx = 2
7. Process element 0: 0 > 0 is false → skip
8. Return [5, 2]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Iteration with Conditional Check
ladders.desc: Iterate through an array and perform actions based on whether elements satisfy a condition.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves traversing an array element by element and checking a condition for each element. The intuition is simple: arrays are linear data structures, so we process them sequentially from start to end. For each position, we examine the element and decide what to do based on whether it meets our condition. The algorithm is straightforward: initialize any needed variables, then for i from 0 to n-1: current = arr[i], if condition(current) is true: do_action1, else: do_action2. Time complexity is O(n) for the linear scan. Space complexity is O(1) if we only use a fixed number of variables. This connects to L3 as it forms the core loop of conditional copying. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds, or incorrectly implementing the condition. The real code pattern shows the basic for-loop structure with conditional logic inside.

examples.input: Array: [1, 0, 3, 0, 12], Condition: element != 0, Action: count it
examples.trace:
Array: [1, 0, 3, 0, 12], Condition: element != 0
1. Initialize count = 0
2. i = 0, element = 1: 1 != 0 is true → count = 1
3. i = 1, element = 0: 0 != 0 is false → count stays 1
4. i = 2, element = 3: 3 != 0 is true → count = 2
5. i = 3, element = 0: 0 != 0 is false → count stays 2
6. i = 4, element = 12: 12 != 0 is true → count = 3
7. Return count = 3

examples.input: Array: [0, 5, 0, 2, 0], Condition: element > 0, Action: print element
examples.trace:
Array: [0, 5, 0, 2, 0], Condition: element > 0
1. i = 0, element = 0: 0 > 0 is false → do nothing
2. i = 1, element = 5: 5 > 0 is true → print 5
3. i = 2, element = 0: 0 > 0 is false → do nothing
4. i = 3, element = 2: 2 > 0 is true → print 2
5. i = 4, element = 0: 0 > 0 is false → do nothing
6. Output would show printed values: 5, 2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Basics and Element Access
ladders.desc: Fundamental concepts of arrays: creation, indexing, and accessing elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the absolute basics of arrays needed to understand the problem. An array is a linear data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element has an index (position) starting from 0. The intuition is that arrays allow us to store multiple values of the same type and access them quickly by their position. Key operations: 1. Creating an array of size n. 2. Accessing element at index i using array[i]. 3. Modifying element at index i using array[i] = value. 4. Finding length of array. Arrays have fixed size once created. Time complexity for access by index is O(1). Space complexity is O(n) to store n elements. This connects to L4 as all array iteration builds upon these basic operations. Common beginner mistakes include confusing 0-based indexing (thinking index 1 is first element), or trying to access indices outside array bounds. The real code pattern shows declaration, initialization, and basic access operations.

examples.input: Create array of size 3, set values, access elements
examples.trace:
1. Create array of size 3: arr = [_, _, _]
2. Set arr[0] = 5: arr = [5, _, _]
3. Set arr[1] = 10: arr = [5, 10, _]
4. Set arr[2] = 15: arr = [5, 10, 15]
5. Access arr[0] = 5
6. Access arr[1] = 10
7. Access arr[2] = 15
8. Final array: [5, 10, 15]

Given array: [2, 4, 6, 8]
1. Length = 4 (indices 0, 1, 2, 3)
2. Access arr[0] = 2
3. Access arr[1] = 4
4. Access arr[2] = 6
5. Access arr[3] = 8
6. All elements accessed in order
